plan: - Design and fabricate a tubular steel spaceframe chassis for structural integrity, incorporating mounting points for all powertrain, suspension, and control components. Develop composite aerodynamic body panels for drag reduction and cooling flow. - Integrate a PEM fuel cell stack, lithium battery pack, and DC/DC converter into the powertrain. Implement electric motors for rear-wheel drive, connected via a fixed-ratio gearbox. - Implement a high-pressure hydrogen storage system using a Type IV composite-wound pressure vessel, cascade regulators, and a solenoid shutoff valve, all within a ventilated, leak-sensor-monitored compartment. - Install an autonomous sensor suite including stereo cameras, LiDAR, and IMU, connected to a GPU-accelerated system-on-module for perception, SLAM, path planning, and model predictive control. - Develop and integrate a comprehensive safety architecture: independent Emergency Brake System, Isolation Monitoring Device, and Brake System Plausibility Device, all communicating via CAN bus. - Design custom mounts and enclosures for sensors, the onboard computer, and power electronics, potentially using 3D printing for specialized geometries.

originalPrompt:
The car is a single-seat open-wheel racing prototype built around a hydrogen powertrain. At the back, a polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) fuel cell stack — roughly the size of a large carry-on bag — converts compressed hydrogen and ambient air into electricity through an electrochemical reaction, producing only water vapor as exhaust. Because a fuel cell alone can't deliver the sharp bursts of power a racing car demands during hard acceleration, a lithium battery pack sits alongside it as a buffer. The fuel cell runs at steady state, continuously charging the pack; the pack handles the spikes. A DC/DC converter bridges the two, keeping voltage levels compatible across the system. From there, power flows to one or two electric motors driving the rear wheels through a fixed-ratio gearbox — no clutch, no gear changes, instant torque from standstill.
The hydrogen itself is stored in a Type IV composite-wound pressure vessel — carbon fiber wrapped over a polymer liner — at 350 bar, roughly 5,000 psi. A cascade of pressure regulators steps that down to the few bar the fuel cell stack actually operates at. The entire hydrogen circuit is enclosed in a ventilated compartment with electrochemical leak sensors that trigger an automatic solenoid shutoff valve if concentration approaches even a fraction of the lower flammability limit.
Wrapped around all of this is a tubular steel spaceframe chassis — the structural skeleton that carries the driver, all powertrain components, and the suspension geometry. Aerodynamic bodywork in composite panels keeps drag low and channels cooling air to the fuel cell and power electronics. The driver sits reclined in a carbon fiber seat, helmet level with the roll hoop, connected to the car through a steering wheel, brake pedal, and throttle.
On top of all the above, the car is also autonomous. A stereo camera mounted above the nose cone identifies the colored cones that define the track boundary — yellow on the left, blue on the right, orange at the start line. A LiDAR unit adds a three-dimensional point cloud of the environment for depth and redundancy. An IMU measures the car's acceleration and rotation at high frequency. All of this feeds into an onboard computer — a GPU-accelerated system-on-module the size of a thick paperback — running a perception pipeline that detects cones, a SLAM algorithm that builds a map of the track in real time, a path planner that computes the racing line through that map, and a model predictive controller that translates that line into steering, throttle, and brake commands sent over the CAN bus, the car's internal electrical nervous system.
The safety architecture runs in parallel with everything else. An Emergency Brake System with its own independent power source can stop the car regardless of any software state. An Isolation Monitoring Device watches continuously for any fault current path between the high-voltage system and the chassis. A Brake System Plausibility Device cross-checks sensor readings to detect conflicting inputs. None of this is optional — Formula Student rules require all of it, and the car won't pass technical inspection without them.
What makes this combination unusual is the last part. Hydrogen fuel cell Formula Student cars exist — TU Delft's Forze team has been building them for over a decade. Fully autonomous Formula Student cars exist too, in the driverless discipline. But a car that is simultaneously a hydrogen fuel cell vehicle and a fully autonomous system is essentially uncharted territory in the competition. That combination is what AGÜ FCEV is trying to build.

subSteps.title: Weld and deburr tubular steel chassis and roll hoop sections
3D print all PETG and ABS component mounts
Fabricate and prepare structural mounts for fuel cell, battery, and steering actuator
Assemble and test pedal assemblies and steering column linkage
Form and trim composite bodywork and hydrogen enclosure panels
Integrate and seal hydrogen regulators and solenoid valve within the hydrogen enclosure
Crimp and connect all low voltage power wiring from LV DC/DC converter to modules and sensors
Assemble and connect high voltage power lines, including contactors and main battery/fuel cell paths
Solder and connect CAN bus wiring between transceivers and all CAN-capable devices
Wire Ethernet connections for autonomous computer, LiDAR, and main ECU
Connect USB and SPI data lines for cameras, IMU, and autonomous computer
Wire analog and digital sensor inputs and actuator control signals to respective MCUs
Apply heat shrink, secure connectors, and label all wiring harnesses
Verify continuity and proper voltage on all low voltage power rails
Power up main ECU and autonomous computer; install operating systems and drivers
Load initial firmware onto all MCUs and program pin assignments
Test CAN bus communication for all connected devices and verify message integrity
Perform initial power-up and functional test of the hydrogen management system and related sensors
Verify sensor readings (LiDAR, Camera, IMU, pressure, position) and actuator responses (steering, throttle, brake)
Test emergency stop system and high voltage contactor operation
Mount driver's seat, pedal assemblies, and steering column to chassis
Install suspension arms, gearbox, and wheel assemblies to the chassis
Mount all electronic components and their respective mounts to the chassis or designated locations
Install hydrogen pressure vessel and enclosure, connecting to primary and secondary regulators
Route and secure all wiring harnesses, applying strain relief and vibration dampening
Fasten all aerodynamic bodywork panels to the chassis, ensuring proper alignment
Perform final system calibration, functional checks, and safety architecture validation

projectDescription: An autonomous, open-wheel racing prototype combines a hydrogen fuel cell and battery hybrid powertrain with a tubular steel chassis and composite bodywork. Advanced sensors like LiDAR and stereo cameras guide its driverless operation, backed by comprehensive Formula Student safety systems.

Design notes: hydrogen powertrain, autonomous system, tubular steel chassis, composite bodywork, open-wheel racing, Formula Student rules, safety architecture

Electrical components (current): 4x mcu: Main Vehicle Control Unit, Autonomous Onboard Computer, Emergency Brake System MCU, Brake System Plausibility Device; 9x sensor: Front Stereo Camera, LiDAR Unit, High-Frequency IMU, Hydrogen Leak Sensor Front +5 more; 5x module: Fuel Cell Management Unit, Isolation Monitoring Device, CAN Bus Transceiver (Main), CAN Bus Transceiver (Autonomous) +1 more; 7x power: High Voltage Battery Management System, High Voltage DC/DC Converter (FC to Battery), Left Rear Wheel Motor Controller, Right Rear Wheel Motor Controller +3 more; 5x actuator: Hydrogen Shutoff Solenoid Valve, Steering Actuator (Servo Motor), Throttle Pedal Actuator, Brake Pedal Actuator +1 more; Safety Status Light Beacon (display, 100x70x50mm)

Mechanical/structural parts (current): 11x structural: Tubular Steel Spaceframe Chassis, Roll Hoop, Carbon Fiber Driver Seat, Type IV Hydrogen Pressure Vessel +7 more; 6x enclosure: Front Aerodynamic Bodywork Panel, Left Mid Aerodynamic Bodywork Panel, Right Mid Aerodynamic Bodywork Panel, Rear Aerodynamic Bodywork Panel +2 more; 11x mechanism: Fixed-Ratio Gearbox, Primary Hydrogen Pressure Regulator, Secondary Hydrogen Pressure Regulator, Racing Steering Wheel +7 more; 30x 3d_printed: Stereo Camera Mount, LiDAR Unit Mount, IMU Mount, Autonomous Computer Mount +26 more

Assembly: Tubular Steel Spaceframe Chassis → Roll Hoop (welded/bolted); Tubular Steel Spaceframe Chassis → Carbon Fiber Driver Seat (bolted); Tubular Steel Spaceframe Chassis → Front Aerodynamic Bodywork Panel (fastened/quick-release); Tubular Steel Spaceframe Chassis → Left Mid Aerodynamic Bodywork Panel (fastened/quick-release); Tubular Steel Spaceframe Chassis → Right Mid Aerodynamic Bodywork Panel (fastened/quick-release); Tubular Steel Spaceframe Chassis → Rear Aerodynamic Bodywork Panel (fastened/quick-release); Tubular Steel Spaceframe Chassis → Composite Nose Cone (fastened/quick-release); Tubular Steel Spaceframe Chassis → Fixed-Ratio Gearbox (bolted); Tubular Steel Spaceframe Chassis → Type IV Hydrogen Pressure Vessel (strapped/clamped to mounts); Type IV Hydrogen Pressure Vessel → Primary Hydrogen Pressure Regulator (high-pressure fitting); Primary Hydrogen Pressure Regulator → Secondary Hydrogen Pressure Regulator (plumbing/fitting); Tubular Steel Spaceframe Chassis → Hydrogen System Ventilated Enclosure (bolted/sealed); Hydrogen System Ventilated Enclosure → Type IV Hydrogen Pressure Vessel (encloses); Hydrogen System Ventilated Enclosure → Primary Hydrogen Pressure Regulator (encloses); Hydrogen System Ventilated Enclosure → Secondary Hydrogen Pressure Regulator (encloses) (+81 more connections)

tools: Hex key set (metric: M3, M4, M5, M6)
Soldering iron with fine and standard tips
Fiber optic crimping tool (if applicable to some advanced CAN/Ethernet setups)
Specialized hydrogen plumbing tools (e.g., swaging tools for high-pressure fittings)
assumptions: Proficiency in welding tubular steel chassis components
Basic understanding of composite material handling and finishing
Experience with high voltage (400V) electrical systems and safety protocols
Familiarity with CAN bus communication and network topology
Proficiency in soldering and crimping various connector types
Basic embedded programming skills for firmware loading and debugging
Familiarity with Linux-based operating systems for the Autonomous Onboard Computer
Access to a well-ventilated workshop for fabrication and assembly
